title: Chapter 9 — Building a Personal Claude Workflow
CHAPTER 9 —
BUILDING A PERSONAL CLAUDE WORKFLOW
segments.body_html: A personal Claude workflow is not a folder of clever prompts. It is a repeatable operating loop: diagnose the task, choo

beats.text: A personal Claude workflow is not a folder of clever prompts. It is a repeatable operating loop: diagnose the task, choose the surface, prepare context, specify the output, supervise the work, and verify before use. Good workflow design starts with task inventory, not tool optimization. Projects, memory, and personalization features support continuity , but they are not substitutes for clear thinking about what Claude should and should not do. The goal is a small set of disciplined, auditable workflows, not maximum automation.

segments.label: 01 / 13 · HOOK

title: OPENING SCENE
segments.body_html:
You open your laptop Monday morning with fourteen browser tabs still pinned from last week. Somewhere in seven different

Opening Scene — This is not a Claude problem. It is a workflow problem. Claude can hold context, remember instructions, organize files,

beats.text: You open your laptop Monday morning with fourteen browser tabs still pinned from last week. Somewhere in seven different Claude conversations , none of them named, some marked "new chat" , are a draft you liked, a source list you never verified, and a summary that probably had an error you meant to fix. You cannot find any of them. You start over. This is not a Claude problem. It is a workflow problem. Claude can hold context, remember instructions, organize files, and run multi-step tasks.

segments.label: 02 / 13 · OPENING SCENE

title: THE CORE PROBLEM:
HABIT WITHOUT SYSTEM
segments.body_html:
Most people who use Claude regularly have developed habits without developing a system. They ask Claude for summaries th

The Core Problem: Habit Without System — That works well enough for low-stakes, one-off tasks. It breaks down for recurring work: the weekly report, the literatu

beats.text: Most people who use Claude regularly have developed habits without developing a system. They ask Claude for summaries the same way they asked a search engine for articles: open a window, type a question, get an answer, close the tab. The conversation is gone. The context is gone. The verification note, if there was one, is gone. That works well enough for low-stakes, one-off tasks.

segments.label: 03 / 13 · THE CORE PROBLEM: HABIT WITHOU

title: THE OPERATING
LOOP.
segments.body_html:
·Name the work. What recurring task are you building a workflow for? Be specific. "Writing" is not a workstream. "First-d
·Choose the surface. Claude AI (chat) for thinking, planning, drafting, and explanation. Claude Code for codebase and scr
Prepare context. What does Claude need to know to do this task well? What source files, prior outputs, constraints, or asensitive data, unpublished material, confidential names?
·Specify the output. What artifact should Claude produce? What format, length, tone, and verification standard apply?
·Let Claude propose a plan. For multi-step tasks, ask Claude to summarize its plan before it acts. This is an approval ga

beats.text: A personal Claude workflow has eight steps, run in sequence for each recurring task: Name the work. What recurring task are you building a workflow for? Be specific. "Writing" is not a workstream. "First-draft client briefing notes from raw interview transcripts" is.

segments.label: 04 / 13 · THE OPERATING LOOP

title: THE SCOPE
STACK.
segments.body_html:
Account instructions — Stable personal preferencesyour usual role, tone defaults, citation style, privacy basics
Project instructions — Stable project rulesaudience, output format, approved sources, access limits, verification standard
Task prompt — Current jobthe specific ask, context for this instance, any one-time constraints
Human gate — Your verification actionwhat you check before the output leaves your desk

beats.text: One of the most common workflow mistakes is cramming every preference, constraint, and rule into every prompt. This produces long, fragile prompts that are hard to maintain and easy to misread. A better design is a scope stack: stable preferences live at the top of the stack, task-specific instructions live at the bottom.

segments.label: 05 / 13 · THE SCOPE STACK

title: PROJECTS:
THE ORGANIZING UNIT
segments.body_html:
·You run this kind of work repeatedly
·The work benefits from persistent context (background, style, source list, constraints)
·The output has a verification path
·A brief description of the workstream and its purpose
·Stable instructions (what Claude should do, what it should not do, what format to use)

beats.text: A project is the right organizational unit for recurring work. A project gives you a separate workspace, a shared knowledge base, persistent instructions, and a place to keep related conversations and files (Anthropic, "How can I create and manage projects?" Claude Help Center, March 16, 2026 ). Create one project for each workstream that meets all three of these conditions:

segments.label: 06 / 13 · PROJECTS: THE ORGANIZING UNIT

title: MEMORY:
USEFUL AND RISKY
segments.body_html:
Claude's memory features allow context to persist across conversations. This can reduce friction for recurring users: yo

Memory: Useful and Risky — Memory is useful. It is also a privacy surface and a staleness risk.

beats.text: Claude's memory features allow context to persist across conversations. This can reduce friction for recurring users: you do not have to re-explain your role, your audience, or your preferences every session (Anthropic, "Use Claude's chat search and memory to build on previous context," Claude Help Center, March 16, 2026 ). Memory is useful. It is also a privacy surface and a staleness risk.

segments.label: 07 / 13 · MEMORY: USEFUL AND RISKY

title: TASK CLASSIFICATION:
THE WORKFLOW FOUNDATION
segments.body_html:
·What does Claude produce? (Text, code, file, plan, analysis, something else)
·Can I verify this output without Claude's help? (Yes, partially, no)
·What is the risk if this output is wrong? (Low, medium, high)
·What happens if a sensitive piece of information ends up in the output by mistake?
Claude AI (chat)Thinking, drafting, explanation, critique, brainstorming. You need fast conversation with human review before anything l

beats.text: Before you design any workflow, you need to know what you are building it for. The most reliable way to do that is a task inventory. List ten to fifteen recurring tasks in your work. For each one, answer four questions:

segments.label: 08 / 13 · TASK CLASSIFICATION: THE WORKF

title: THE PERSONAL
WORKFLOW CANVAS.
segments.body_html:
Workstream name — What is this workflow for?
Claude surface — Chat, Code, Cowork, or human-only?
Allowed inputs — What context and files can go in?
Forbidden inputs — What must never be included?

beats.text: Use this canvas to design one workflow. Fill it out before you start using Claude on a new recurring task. Fill this out for one workflow before building it. Revisit it after the first three runs.

segments.label: 09 / 13 · THE PERSONAL WORKFLOW CANVAS

title: COMMON MISTAKES
segments.body_html: Treating a master prompt as a workflow.→Using memory as a substitute for context design.→Loading project instructions with every possible rule.→Optimizing the workflow before diagnosing the task.→Setting a workflow and forgetting it.

beats.text: Treating a master prompt as a workflow. A long, ambitious prompt is not a workflow. It is a prompt. A workflow includes context design, project setup, verification, and improvement cycles. Using memory as a substitute for context design. Memory reduces re-typing, but it does not think for you about what Claude should know. A stale memory can silently corrupt a workflow.

segments.label: 10 / 13 · COMMON MISTAKES

Bridge to Chapter 10 — The capstone takes one real project — the one you identified in Exercise 4 — through all three Claude surfaces, with exp

beats.text: This chapter gave you the design template. Chapter 10 asks you to use it. The capstone takes one real project , the one you identified in Exercise 4 , through all three Claude surfaces, with explicit routing decisions, approval gates, a verification log, and a reflection on what changed after review. It is where the pieces from Chapters 1 through 9 meet in practice.
